summary: Typosquat of @juggle/resize-observer ships cross-platform clipboard-hijacking clipper with persistence
details: Package name mimics the legitimate @juggle/resize-observer polyfill and copies its README verbatim, but ships an entirely different payload. The npm-install.js postinstall script installs a bundled Python package 'clipboard_guardian' via `pip install --break-system-packages` (overriding PEP 668), and attempts privileged `sudo apt-get install -y xclip` (also pacman/dnf) to install build dependencies without consent. It then registers cross-platform persistence under names chosen to impersonate OS components: Linux systemd user unit `python3-dbus-helper.service` (with `Restart=always`) under `~/.config/systemd/user/`, macOS LaunchAgent `com.apple.python.runtime.plist`, and Windows Scheduled Task `PyRuntimeBroker`. The Python daemon (guardian.py) monitors the user's clipboard and, when a cryptocurrency address is detected (BTC/ETH/SOL/XMR and 30+ chains), silently substitutes it with hardcoded attacker-controlled wallet addresses (e.g., ETH `0x450c0E58Fc2ba03632d3F5780ad8C966648B6F18`, Monero `42zhAidVhP7QETk83JAspS59ASALSHFio44vmu6MdDCz15c...`), redirecting any cryptocurrency transfer the user attempts. The daemon also uses setproctitle to disguise itself with the same fake-system names, and enumerates running processes (Task Manager, Process Hacker, Activity Monitor, htop, btop, etc.) via psutil to pause its behavior when an investigator is watching. Multiple independent block signals: typosquat with divergent payload, install-time privilege escalation, cross-platform persistence under impersonating names, financial-theft clipboard hijacking, and anti-analysis evasion.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
